title: Build Your First Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) System
audience: beginner to intermediate developers who understand basic Python but are new to RAG systems

chapters.title: Set Up Your RAG Development Environment
chapters.chapter_goal: Install necessary Python libraries and set up a basic project structure to begin building the RAG system.

sections.title: Understanding the RAG Architecture: A High-Level Overview
sections.goal: Introduce the core components of a RAG system and how they interact, providing a mental model for the project.
key_questions: What are the main components of a RAG system?
How do these components work together to answer a question?
What is the role of each component in our project?

sections.title: Installing Core Python Libraries
sections.goal: Install the essential Python packages required for building the RAG system, including LangChain and an LLM client.
key_questions: What Python packages do we need for RAG?
How do we install these packages using pip?
What are the potential issues during installation and how to resolve them?
sections.builds_on: Understanding the RAG Architecture: A High-Level Overview

sections.title: Setting Up Your LLM API Key and Environment Variables
sections.goal: Configure access to a Large Language Model (LLM) by setting up API keys securely using environment variables.
key_questions: Why do we need an API key for LLMs?
How can we securely manage API keys?
How do we load environment variables in Python?

sections.title: Creating Your First RAG Script: A Minimal Example
sections.goal: Write a basic Python script that demonstrates the initial setup and readiness to integrate RAG components.
key_questions: What does a minimal RAG script look like?
How can we test our environment setup with a simple script?
What are the next steps to add functionality?
sections.builds_on: Setting Up Your LLM API Key and Environment Variables

chapters.project_milestone: A Python project with essential libraries (e.g., LangChain, OpenAI/Hugging Face client) installed and a basic script ready to run.

chapters.title: Ingest and Chunk Your Documents
chapters.chapter_goal: Implement the document loading and text splitting logic to prepare your documents for the RAG system.

sections.title: Loading Documents from Files
sections.goal: Learn how to load text content from various file types into a format suitable for processing.
key_questions: What are common document formats for RAG systems?
How can we programmatically load text from files in Python?
What libraries are available for document loading?
How do we handle potential errors during file loading?

sections.title: Understanding Text Chunking Strategies
sections.goal: Explore different methods for splitting large documents into smaller, semantically meaningful chunks.
key_questions: Why is chunking necessary for RAG systems?
What are the trade-offs between fixed-size chunking and recursive chunking?
How can we preserve context across chunks?
What are common chunk sizes and why?

sections.title: Implementing Recursive Character Text Splitting
sections.goal: Write Python code to implement a recursive character text splitter, a common and effective chunking technique.
key_questions: How does recursive character splitting work in practice?
What are the key parameters for a recursive splitter (e.g., chunk size, overlap)?
How can we use libraries like LangChain to simplify this process?
How do we ensure chunks are not too small or too large?

sections.title: Putting It All Together: A Document Ingestion Script
sections.goal: Combine document loading and chunking into a single script that prepares documents for the next stage of the RAG pipeline.
key_questions: How do we integrate the document loading and chunking functions?
What is the expected output of this script?
How can we test our script with sample documents?
What are common errors encountered during this stage and how to debug them?

chapters.project_milestone: A script that can load text files and split them into manageable chunks, ready for embedding.

chapters.title: Create Document Embeddings and a Vector Store
chapters.chapter_goal: Generate vector embeddings for document chunks and store them in a simple in-memory or local vector database.

sections.title: Understanding Embeddings: Turning Text into Numbers
sections.goal: Explain what text embeddings are, why they are necessary for RAG, and how they represent semantic meaning.
key_questions: What is a text embedding?
Why do we need embeddings for RAG?
How do embedding models capture semantic similarity?
What are the trade-offs between different embedding models (e.g., size, performance, cost)?

sections.title: Generating Embeddings with an Open-Source Model
sections.goal: Implement code to generate vector embeddings for text chunks using a pre-trained, open-source embedding model.
key_questions: How can we load and use an embedding model in Python?
What is the input and output of an embedding model?
How do we process multiple text chunks efficiently?
What are common parameters to consider when generating embeddings?
sections.builds_on: Understanding Embeddings: Turning Text into Numbers

sections.title: Choosing and Setting Up a Vector Store
sections.goal: Introduce the concept of a vector store and set up a simple, in-memory or local file-based vector store for our embeddings.
key_questions: What is a vector store and why is it different from a traditional database?
What are the options for vector stores (in-memory, local, cloud)?
How do we choose a vector store for a beginner project?
How do we initialize and configure a basic vector store?
sections.builds_on: Generating Embeddings with an Open-Source Model

sections.title: Storing and Indexing Embeddings
sections.goal: Write code to add the generated document embeddings to the chosen vector store and ensure they are indexed for efficient retrieval.
key_questions: How do we add embeddings and their associated text to the vector store?
What metadata should we store alongside embeddings?
How does the vector store index embeddings for fast searching?
What are the common methods for adding data to a vector store?
sections.builds_on: Choosing and Setting Up a Vector Store

chapters.project_milestone: A working RAG component that converts text chunks into numerical embeddings and stores them in a searchable vector store.

chapters.title: Implement the Retrieval Component
chapters.chapter_goal: Build the retriever that queries the vector store using a user's question to find the most relevant document chunks.

sections.title: Understanding the Retriever's Role
sections.goal: Explain the purpose and function of the retriever component within a RAG system, emphasizing its role in finding relevant information.
key_questions: What is a retriever in the context of RAG?
Why is effective retrieval crucial for RAG performance?
How does the retriever bridge the gap between a user's question and the document corpus?

sections.title: Embedding the User's Query
sections.goal: Implement the process of converting a user's natural language question into a vector embedding using the same model used for document embeddings.
key_questions: How do we represent a user's question in a way the vector database can understand?
Which embedding model should be used for the query, and why?
What are the steps to generate an embedding for a given text string?

sections.title: Querying the Vector Store
sections.goal: Write the code to query the vector database using the query embedding and retrieve the top K most similar document chunks.
key_questions: How do we connect to the vector database?
What is the specific query command to find nearest neighbors?
How do we specify the number of results (K) to retrieve?
What information do we get back from the vector store query?

sections.title: Putting it Together: The Retriever Class
sections.goal: Encapsulate the query embedding and vector store querying logic into a reusable Python class.
key_questions: How can we organize the retriever logic into a clean, object-oriented structure?
What parameters should the Retriever class accept (e.g., embedding model, vector store client)?
How does the `retrieve` method orchestrate the embedding and querying steps?
How can we test this retriever class with sample questions?

sections.title: Debugging Common Retrieval Issues
sections.goal: Identify and troubleshoot common problems encountered during the retrieval phase, such as irrelevant results or no results found.
key_questions: What are the signs of a poorly performing retriever?
How can we inspect the quality of retrieved chunks?
What are common causes for poor retrieval (e.g., embedding model mismatch, incorrect K value, poor chunking)?
How can we adjust parameters or data to improve retrieval accuracy?
sections.builds_on: Putting it Together: The Retriever Class

chapters.project_milestone: A functional retriever that takes a question, generates its embedding, and returns the top N most similar document chunks from the vector store.

chapters.title: Integrate a Language Model for Generation
chapters.chapter_goal: Connect a generative language model (LLM) to the retriever to generate answers based on the retrieved document chunks and the user's question.

sections.title: Choosing and Setting Up Your Language Model
sections.goal: Introduce the concept of a generative language model (LLM) and guide the reader in selecting and setting up a suitable LLM for their RAG system.
key_questions: What is an LLM and how does it function as the 'generator' in RAG?
What are the different types of LLMs available (e.g., OpenAI, Hugging Face, local models)?
How do we set up API access or load a local model for use in our Python script?
What are the basic parameters to consider when interacting with an LLM (e.g., temperature, max tokens)?

sections.title: Crafting the Prompt for Contextual Answers
sections.goal: Teach the reader how to construct effective prompts that combine the user's question with the retrieved document context to guide the LLM's generation.
key_questions: Why is prompt engineering crucial for RAG systems?
How do we format the prompt to include both the user's question and the retrieved document chunks?
What are common prompt patterns for RAG (e.g., 'Answer the following question based on the provided context:')?
How can we instruct the LLM to only use the provided context and to state if it doesn't know the answer?
sections.builds_on: Choosing and Setting Up Your Language Model

sections.title: Generating Answers with the LLM
sections.goal: Implement the core logic to pass the crafted prompt to the LLM and receive the generated answer, integrating it with the retrieval component.
key_questions: How do we call the LLM API or model with our constructed prompt?
How do we parse the LLM's response to extract the generated answer?
What are potential issues when generating answers (e.g., hallucinations, irrelevant information)?
How can we handle errors or empty responses from the LLM?
sections.builds_on: Crafting the Prompt for Contextual Answers

sections.title: Putting It All Together: The Complete RAG Pipeline
sections.goal: Integrate all previously built components (ingestion, chunking, embeddings, retrieval, LLM generation) into a single, runnable RAG pipeline.
key_questions: How do the retriever and generator components interact in the final pipeline?
How can we structure the code for a clear and modular RAG system?
What are the end-to-end steps from user question to final answer?
How can we test the complete pipeline with a sample question?

chapters.project_milestone: A complete, albeit basic, RAG pipeline that takes a question, retrieves relevant context, and generates an answer using an LLM.

chapters.title: Refine Prompts and Evaluate Basic Performance
chapters.chapter_goal: Learn to craft effective prompts for the LLM and perform simple qualitative evaluations of the RAG system's answers.

sections.title: Understanding Prompt Engineering for RAG
sections.goal: Introduce the fundamental concepts of prompt engineering and explain why it's crucial for RAG systems.
key_questions: What is prompt engineering?
Why is prompt engineering important for RAG?
What are the key elements of a good RAG prompt?
How can prompt structure influence LLM output?

sections.title: Crafting a Dynamic Prompt Template
sections.goal: Develop a flexible prompt template that incorporates retrieved context and the user's question, enabling the LLM to generate informed answers.
key_questions: How can we dynamically insert retrieved documents and the user's question into a prompt?
What are common placeholders or variables used in prompt templates?
How do we instruct the LLM to use the provided context to answer the question?
What are some best practices for structuring the prompt for clarity and effectiveness?

sections.title: Implementing Basic Qualitative Evaluation
sections.goal: Establish a simple, manual process for evaluating the quality of the RAG system's answers, focusing on relevance, accuracy, and completeness.
key_questions: What are the key criteria for evaluating RAG output?
How can we systematically test our RAG system with a set of questions?
What does a good evaluation checklist look like?
How do we compare the LLM's answer against the retrieved context and the expected answer?

sections.title: Iterative Refinement: Prompt and Evaluation Loop
sections.goal: Demonstrate how to use evaluation results to iteratively improve the prompt template and enhance the RAG system's performance.
key_questions: How do we analyze the results of our qualitative evaluation?
What common issues arise from poor prompts or retrieval?
How can we adjust the prompt template to address identified weaknesses?
What is the feedback loop between evaluation and prompt refinement?

chapters.project_milestone: An improved RAG system with a more sophisticated prompt template, and a method for manually assessing the quality of its responses.

running_project: A functional RAG system that can answer questions based on a small set of your own documents.
